_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Botetourt County, Virginia. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the southern gateway county where the Valley meets the Great Road to Tennessee and Kentucky.

current_courthouse.location: Fincastle, VA — 1 West Main Street (Fincastle has been the county seat since 1770; the 1848 courthouse remains in use with renovations)

previous_courthouses.fate: Botetourt County was formed from Augusta County in 1770. The original courthouse was a frontier structure. Records from this era cover an immense territory — the original Botetourt County extended to the Mississippi River, encompassing much of modern Kentucky and West Virginia.

previous_courthouses.location: Fincastle — frame and later brick courthouse
previous_courthouses.fate: Served through the early Republic. Records transferred to the 1848 building intact.

record_losses.cause: Civil War — Hunter's raid through the southern Valley
record_losses.description: Hunter's Union force passed through Botetourt County in June 1864 en route to Lynchburg. Fincastle, set back from the main road, suffered less damage than towns along the direct line of march. The courthouse records survived essentially intact. Botetourt County's record collection is one of the most complete in western Virginia, with continuous series from 1770 — a remarkable survival for a frontier county that originally encompassed territory stretching to the Mississippi.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Botetourt County's records are intact from 1770, covering the era when the county was Virginia's gateway to the west. If {ancestor_name} passed through Botetourt on the way to Kentucky, their earliest western records may be at Fincastle — deeds, court orders, or militia rolls filed before the family crossed the mountains.
record_losses.source: Kegley, Mary B. Early Adventurers on the Western Waters. 2 vols. Orange, VA: Green Publishers, 1980–1982.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation. The early deed books cover transactions across a vast territory extending west to the Ohio River.
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Pre-1770 deeds are in Augusta County at Staunton. Some early deeds reference land in areas that became Kentucky or West Virginia counties.
deeds.repository: Botetourt County Circuit Court, Fincastle; Library of Virginia microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com Virginia land records; Library of Virginia digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — continuous from formation. Early estate inventories are particularly valuable for documenting frontier material life.
wills_estates.gaps: Some loose estate papers from the 1770s are fragmentary. Bound will books are intact.
wills_estates.repository: Botetourt County Circuit Court; Library of Virginia
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Library of Virginia digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from county formation. Minister returns are scattered before 1853.
marriages.gaps: Some frontier-era marriages performed by itinerant ministers were never recorded civilly. After daughter counties were carved from Botetourt, some marriages were mistakenly recorded in the new county rather than the parent.
marriages.repository: Botetourt County Circuit Court; Library of Virginia microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — County Court order books are nearly continuous from the first sessions. The early books are frontier records of extraordinary genealogical value.
court_minutes.gaps: The earliest order books document not just Botetourt but the entire western Virginia frontier — road petitions, Indian trade regulations, militia musters, estate settlements, and servant/slave transactions. As daughter counties were created (1772–1790s), the geographic scope of the records narrowed.
court_minutes.repository: Botetourt County Circuit Court; Library of Virginia microfilm
court_minutes.online_access: Library of Virginia microfilm; some transcriptions at Kegley's publications

vital_records.completeness: Poor to fair — compliance was low in Botetourt's rural and mountain communities. The 1853–1896 registers are very incomplete. Registration resumed 1912.
vital_records.repository: Virginia Department of Health, Office of Vital Records; Library of Virginia for pre-1896 registers

substitute_sources.description: Botetourt County was formed from Augusta County in 1770. All records before that date are at Staunton, covered in Chalkley's Chronicles.
substitute_sources.repository: Augusta County Circuit Court, Staunton; Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for any Botetourt family tracing back before 1770. The earliest settlers along the Great Road and James River appeared first in Augusta County records.
substitute_sources.source: Chalkley, Lyman. Chronicles of the Scotch-Irish Settlement in Virginia. 3 vols. Rosslyn, VA: Commonwealth Printing, 1912.

substitute_sources.description: Botetourt County's iron furnaces — Eagle Rock, Longdale, and others — maintained account books, time books, and production records. These document both free and enslaved workers.
substitute_sources.repository: Virginia Museum of History and Culture; Duke University Special Collections; private collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Furnace records document enslaved ironworkers by name and skill in many cases. For free workers, they record wages and supplies. The iron furnace records are sometimes more detailed than agricultural records for the same era.
substitute_sources.source: Dew, Charles B. Bond of Iron: Master and Slave at Buffalo Forge. New York: W.W. Norton, 1994.

substitute_sources.source_type: James River and Kanawha Canal records
substitute_sources.description: The canal's western terminus was at Buchanan in Botetourt County. Canal company records document tolls, cargo, boat registrations, and worker employment.
substitute_sources.repository: Virginia Museum of History and Culture; Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Canal records document commerce and employment along the James River corridor. Workers (including enslaved laborers who built the canal) may appear in these records when they appear nowhere else.
substitute_sources.source: Dunaway, Wayland Fuller. History of the James River and Kanawha Company. New York: Columbia University Press, 1922.

substitute_sources.source_type: Kegley's Early Adventurers on the Western Waters
substitute_sources.description: Mary Kegley's multi-volume transcription and analysis of early Botetourt County and southwestern Virginia records provides abstracted court orders, deeds, and family data from the frontier era.
substitute_sources.repository: Most Virginia research libraries; some digitized
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Kegley's work is the equivalent of Chalkley's Chronicles for the Botetourt County area — essential for tracing families who passed through on the way to Kentucky or who settled in the southern Valley.
substitute_sources.source: Kegley, Mary B. Early Adventurers on the Western Waters. 2 vols. Orange, VA: Green Publishers, 1980–1982.

narrative_hooks: Botetourt County's records begin in 1770 and originally covered territory stretching to the Mississippi — if {ancestor_name} headed west toward Kentucky, the first record of that journey may be a deed or court appearance at Fincastle.
The courthouse at Fincastle has held records continuously since 1770, surviving Hunter's raid and every other disruption — {ancestor_name}'s deeds and wills are almost certainly still in the vault.

If {ancestor_name} worked at one of Botetourt's iron furnaces, the furnace account books may survive in university or private collections — and those records sometimes name enslaved workers individually when plantation records do not.
Before 1770, {ancestor_name}'s Botetourt County records are at Staunton in Augusta County — and Chalkley's Chronicles is the starting point for finding them.

The James River canal terminus at Buchanan left records that document {ancestor_name}'s connection to the outside world — tolls paid, cargo shipped, and workers employed on the waterway that linked Botetourt to Richmond.

Botetourt County was the mother county for much of Kentucky — its court order books from the 1770s document families in transit, stopping long enough to file a deed or settle an estate before crossing the mountains.
source_references: Kegley, Mary B. Early Adventurers on the Western Waters. 2 vols. Orange, VA: Green Publishers, 1980–1982.
Dew, Charles B. Bond of Iron: Master and Slave at Buffalo Forge. New York: W.W. Norton, 1994.
Dunaway, Wayland Fuller. History of the James River and Kanawha Company. New York: Columbia University Press, 1922.
Chalkley, Lyman. Chronicles of the Scotch-Irish Settlement in Virginia. 3 vols. Rosslyn, VA: Commonwealth Printing, 1912.
Heatwole, John L. The Burning: Sheridan in the Shenandoah Valley. Charlottesville: Rockbridge Publishing, 1998.
Library of Virginia. A Guide to Virginia County and City Records on Microfilm. Richmond, various editions.
